stories.title: Online tool connects farmers with available land to cattle ranchers in need of grazing area after wildfires
stories.summary: After Nebraska wildfires burned 800,000+ acres, the Nebraska Grazing Exchange launched a free online tool connecting ranchers who lost grazing land with farmers who have space available. The platform has drawn offers from people across the country, helping ranchers relocate entire herds and avoid economic collapse.
stories.extended_summary: Some ranchers lost 70-80% of their summer grazing range in a single wildfire. Now they're facing a choice: buy expensive hay by the truckload or move their entire herd to another state. The Nebraska Grazing Exchange stepped in with a free online tool that lets farmers offer available land and ranchers send a message to secure it. Offers poured in from all directions—north, south, east, and west—as neighbors rallied to help neighbors. The Nebraska Cattlemen's Association says the tragedy has brought out the best in the agricultural community, with many ranchers relocating hundreds of miles to keep their animals fed.
stories.display_reason: hero always uses image mode (summary always visible)
stories.headline_split: Online tool connects farmers with available land to
fake_endings: urban gardeners seeking community growing spaces
solar panel companies looking for renewable energy sites
stories.fake_headline: Retired Teacher Creates Free Tutoring App That Matches College Students With Elementary Kids in Remote Areas

stories.title: Stocking up the Tiger's Table: Cram the Cruiser aims to support student snack pantries in local schools
stories.summary: What started as a school resource officer keeping chocolate on his desk for students has grown into "Cram the Cruiser," a community-wide food drive to stock snack pantries at two high schools. Officer Phil Anderson says the best part is being there when a student's having a rough day and helping them leave in a better place.
stories.extended_summary: Officer Phil Anderson started buying snacks out of his own pocket for students at Battle Ground High School. It was just a bowl of chocolate at first, but students kept coming back—not just for the food, but for someone to talk to. The pantry became a place where scared, angry, or frustrated kids could sit down and leave feeling better. Now the program has expanded to a second school, and on March 28, the community will fill police cruisers with non-perishable snacks at a Fred Meyer parking lot. Students even named the pantry themselves: the Tiger's Table. Anderson says those daily conversations—the chance to protect and serve by simply being present—are the most rewarding part of the job.
stories.headline_split: Stocking up the Tiger's Table: Cram the Cruiser aims to support
fake_endings: holiday toy drives for families in rural communities
winter coat collections for homeless shelters downtown
stories.fake_headline: Local Bakery Donates Unsold Bread Daily to Feed 300 Ducks at City Sanctuary Park

stories.title: MSU Reclaimed Project board member speaks about work in Southern Africa
stories.summary: MSU's Kappa Sigma fraternity raised over $1 million in two years for the Reclaimed Project, which serves vulnerable children in Lesotho, Africa, and Marks, Mississippi. More than 40 members spent Spring Break in Lesotho, where the nonprofit built a high school that's sending orphans to university in a country where fewer than 16% finish high school.
stories.extended_summary: In Lesotho, less than 16% of people have a high school education. But last year, Reclaimed Project's high school sent seven graduates to university in South Africa—and this year, they're on track to send even more. Jesse Carver, a board member, says it's surreal to hear an orphan in a Third-World country talk about plans for the future, about hopes and dreams. MSU's Kappa Sigma chapter has made Reclaimed Project their philanthropy, raising over $1 million through their annual Charity Bowl in just two years. Members do regular service at The Village educational center in Marks, Mississippi, and 40 of them traveled to Lesotho this Spring Break to work directly with the kids. Carver says once you see how much an education and a little attention can change a child's life, it's impossible not to keep showing up.
stories.headline_split: MSU Reclaimed Project board member speaks about work in
fake_endings: restoring historic buildings across the Midwest region
converting abandoned factories into affordable housing units
stories.fake_headline: Neighborhood Book Club Raises $45,000 to Build Library in Sister City in Guatemala

stories.title: Nahant PD helps raise $7,000 for Make-A-Wish Foundation
stories.summary: Nine police departments plunged into freezing water at Short Beach in Nahant, raising $7,000 for Make-A-Wish. Among the volunteers was Brianna Le Conti, who shared her brother Bryce's medical journey—he suffered his first seizure at six months old and endured years of treatment that shaped her life.
stories.extended_summary: Brianna Le Conti stood on stage at Short Beach and thanked the crowd before sharing a story about her brother Bryce. He was perfectly healthy until his first seizure at six months old in 2001. That moment kicked off years of medical uncertainty and treatment that would shape her entire life. On Sunday, she joined dozens of police officers and regular residents braving harsh winds and freezing water in a polar plunge fundraiser for Make-A-Wish. The Nahant Police Department and eight surrounding departments raised $7,000, but organizers say it wasn't just about the money—it was about showing the community standing behind the children and families who need wishes granted most.
stories.headline_split: Nahant PD helps raise $7,000 for
fake_endings: new playground equipment at elementary school
veteran's memorial restoration in town square

stories.title: NBA Fan Travels The Country To Support Make-A-Wish
stories.summary: Justin Leusner is visiting every NBA arena in the country to raise money for Make-A-Wish, combining his love of basketball with a mission to help kids facing critical illnesses. He's raised over $45,000 so far and is pushing toward a $100,000 goal before his April 1st deadline.
stories.extended_summary: Justin Leusner has three arenas left on his tour of every NBA venue in the country. At each stop, he's raising money for Make-A-Wish—so far, he's brought in over $45,000 and is gunning for $100,000 before his April 1st deadline. Portland fans got to witness his mission on Monday night, as he continues using his love of basketball to bring joy to kids who desperately need it. Fans can follow his journey online and contribute to the cause. Every dollar brings him closer to granting wishes for children facing critical illnesses, turning a personal passion into a cross-country campaign for hope.

former players opening community sports centers

stories.title: Waukesha Boots & Badges: First responders play basketball for charity
stories.summary: Waukesha first responders are trading teamwork for rivalry in the "Boots & Badges" charity basketball game on April 10, with proceeds benefiting Healing Hearts of Southeast Wisconsin and the family of firefighter Matt Baldwin, whose wife Lex is battling aggressive brain cancer. The game brings the community together for local charities while police and fire crews compete on the court.
stories.extended_summary: They work side-by-side during emergencies, but on April 10 at Waukesha South High School, all bets are off. The "Boots & Badges" charity basketball game pits Waukesha police and firefighters against Brookfield and New Berlin first responders in a rivalry that's electric and exciting, according to organizers. This year's beneficiaries hit close to home: Healing Hearts of Southeast Wisconsin and the family of Waukesha firefighter Matt Baldwin, whose wife Lex is fighting aggressive brain cancer. Fire inspector Tom Koerner says it's their number one goal to help the Baldwin family through this fight. Captain Joe Hendricks says it's more than just a game—it's a night where the community can come together and make a real difference for local charities.

fake_endings: new equipment at the youth recreation center

stories.title: Hopeworks Kensington offers paid job training and career help for young adults
stories.summary: Hopeworks Kensington offers paid job training in coding and web design for 17- to 26-year-olds facing barriers to work, helping participants move into full-time jobs averaging $43,000 a year. Tamaya Temple, who studied criminal justice and worked in sales, joined in July and has since learned coding, web design, and public speaking skills.
stories.extended_summary: Tamaya Temple studied criminal justice in college and worked in sales before a friend told her about Hopeworks. She joined in July, unsure what to expect from a program that teaches coding and digital skills. Since then, she's learned coding, web design, communication, and even how to speak confidently in front of large groups. Hopeworks is a nonprofit that serves young adults ages 17-26 who are out of work or looking for a career path. Participants get paid while they train, earning a daily stipend during the learning phase and $15-16 an hour once they move into real work experience. The program blends tech skills with job readiness, helping participants land full-time jobs that average $43,000 a year—careers many once thought were out of reach.
stories.headline_split: Hopeworks Kensington offers paid job training and career help for
fake_endings: formerly incarcerated individuals seeking fresh starts

stories.fake_headline: Hair Salon Offers Free Cuts and Interview Coaching Every Thursday for Job Seekers Over 50

stories.title: Juneau high schoolers travel to Hawaii to help rebuild homes destroyed by Lahaina wildfires
stories.summary: Ten Juneau high school students traveled to Maui to rebuild homes destroyed by the 2023 Lahaina wildfires—some of which were originally built by Juneau students in 2019 and 2020. The House Build program teaches construction skills by building energy-efficient homes in Juneau, and students say the Hawaii trip feels fulfilling because they're learning while helping a community recover.
stories.extended_summary: Jace Kihlmire, a senior at Juneau-Douglas High School, went to Maui for the second time this year with his school's House Build program. He and nine other students spent several days rebuilding homes destroyed by the wildfires that devastated Lahaina in 2023. Some of those homes were originally built by Juneau students in 2019 and 2020, which made the work even more personal. Kihlmire says it feels fulfilling to learn construction skills while knowing you're helping a community heal—it has a positive effect on your mental state. Andy Bullick, who teaches the program, has brought dozens of students on the annual Habitat for Humanity trip over the past decade. He says the real reward is the inspiration students get from traveling somewhere cool, meeting new people, and making a tangible difference.
stories.headline_split: Juneau high schoolers travel to Hawaii to help rebuild homes destroyed by
fake_endings: historic tsunami that devastated coastal villages
volcanic eruption that displaced 200 families
stories.fake_headline: Middle School Robotics Team Builds 15 Wheelchair Ramps for Elderly Neighbors During Summer Break

stories.title: Officers given cop of the month honors for helping deliver baby in Sunset Park
stories.summary: Officers Uzma Malik and Ashley Aviles from the NYPD's 72nd Precinct were named Cops of the Month after helping deliver a healthy baby girl in a Sunset Park apartment at 4:40 a.m. on February 13. The officers were reunited with the baby and her mother at a community council meeting, where they were honored for their quick action.
stories.extended_summary: At 4:40 a.m. on February 13, Officers Uzma Malik and Ashley Aviles arrived at an apartment on Seventh Avenue and 54th Street before the ambulances. Malik put on gloves and grabbed a clean towel, ready to take the baby in her hands. She held the newborn, keeping her safe and warm until EMS arrived to take over. The baby girl was born healthy. On March 12, the officers were honored with Cop of the Month certificates at a community council meeting, where they were reunited with the mother and baby. Deputy Inspector Mobeen Yasin said the officers' actions highlighted their commitment as guardians entrusted with public welfare, not just crime fighters.
stories.headline_split: Officers given cop of the month honors for helping deliver baby in

stories.title: For fifth straight year, women-owned businesses in Manayunk team up to collect donations of period products
stories.summary: For the fifth straight year, 29 women-owned businesses in Manayunk are collecting donations of menstrual and maternal care products through pink crates displayed in their shops. The Manayunk Wingwomen donate everything to Grab-N-Flow, a local mutual aid nonprofit that's opening a new full-time dropoff location next month.
stories.extended_summary: Small pink crates are popping up in 29 women-owned businesses around Manayunk, collecting tampons, pads, diapers, pregnancy tests, and other menstrual and maternal care products. The Manayunk Wingwomen have been running this drive every March since 2021, giving all donations to Grab-N-Flow, a local mutual aid nonprofit founded during the pandemic. Shelley Aragoncillo, who started Grab-N-Flow, says it's her favorite month of the year—she loves watching businesses come together and seeing the excitement of people dropping off products at their favorite store. Anyone who donates gets a free vinyl sticker of the coalition's logo: a maxi pad with its wings spread. Some businesses, like Main Street bistro Blondie, are even adding themed menu items and donating the proceeds. Next month, Grab-N-Flow will open a new full-time dropoff location in the North Light Community Center.

stories.title: Birmingham students travel to Tanzania for Eagle Scout project
stories.summary: Two Birmingham siblings, Eddie and Annie Minor, both Eagle Scout candidates, traveled to Tanzania during school break to deliver donations they collected for their projects. Eddie gathered 120 soccer balls and sports equipment, while Annie collected over 880 art supply kits for children in Tanzania.
stories.extended_summary: A brother and sister packed their bags with hundreds of soccer balls and art kits, then flew to Tanzania to hand-deliver them to kids who'd never owned either. Eddie Minor, a high school junior, collected 120 soccer balls through a pop can drive because he wanted his Eagle Scout project to involve sports. His younger sister Annie, a freshman, gathered 880 craft kits because she loves art. Both are Eagle Scout candidates from Troop 1699 in Birmingham, and both turned their passions into something tangible for children halfway around the world.
stories.headline_split: Birmingham students travel to Tanzania for

stories.title: Intrepid Teens Channel Donations of Old School Uniforms to 1,400 Families, Saving Them $140K
stories.summary: Two San Francisco brothers launched HOPE after seeing a classmate wear shorts on a cold day because he had no clean pants until laundry day. Desmond and Ethan Hua now collect and redistribute donated school uniforms from their garage, serving 1,400 families and saving them an estimated $140,000 while keeping clothes out of landfills.
stories.extended_summary: A boy showed up to school in shorts on a freezing day, and when Desmond and Ethan Hua asked why, he said it was all he had until laundry day. That moment stuck with the brothers. Now their garage is stacked wall-to-wall with plastic bins of neatly folded uniforms—polos, khakis, skirts, sweaters—all organized by size. They've redistributed uniforms to 1,400 families, saving them around $140,000 and keeping hundreds of pounds of textile waste out of landfills. What started as one peer's visible need became a full-scale redistribution operation called HOPE: Help Our Planet Earth.
stories.headline_split: Intrepid Teens Channel Donations of Old School Uniforms to
fake_endings: homeless shelters in three major cities

stories.fake_headline: College Student Creates Free Tutoring Network That Helps 2,300 Kids Ace Their Finals

stories.title: Company that raised minimum salaries to $70,000 is still thriving
stories.summary: Nearly seven years after CEO Dan Price raised all employee salaries at Gravity Payments to a minimum of $70,000—taking a personal pay cut and selling his home to fund it—the company has doubled its workforce and thrived. Critics predicted bankruptcy, but lower turnover and skilled retention proved them wrong.
stories.extended_summary: When Dan Price announced he was raising every employee's salary to at least $70,000, critics lined up to predict his company's collapse. He slashed his own $1 million salary down to $70k, sold his second home, and braced for the fallout. That was nearly seven years ago. Gravity Payments didn't collapse—it doubled in size. Employees stayed longer, gained expertise, and served customers better. Turnover plummeted. Even after losing half its business during the pandemic, Gravity recovered and kept the $70k minimum intact. Price's gamble wasn't just moral—it turned out to be smart business.
stories.headline_split: Company that raised minimum salaries to $70,000
fake_endings: expands to five new cities this quarter

stories.fake_headline: Bakery Goes Debt-Free After Paying Workers Double Industry Standard for Three Years

stories.title: CrossFit isn’t just for the gym-obsessed. In Ohio, it’s reaching incarcerated youth
stories.summary: CrossFit has arrived in Ohio's juvenile corrections facilities, offering incarcerated youth high-intensity workouts designed to build more than muscle. Through the Expanding Horizons program, trained coaches work with around 500 young people on both physical and mental readiness, helping them prepare for life after release.
stories.extended_summary: Jump ropes slap the gym floor at the Circleville Juvenile Correctional Facility as a dozen teens drop into pushups on command. It looks like any CrossFit class—burpees, squats, sweat—but the goals run deeper than muscle gain. Expanding Horizons brings trained coaches into Ohio's three juvenile corrections facilities to work with around 500 incarcerated youth, teaching them not just how to move their bodies but how to rebuild their lives. The program started eight years ago in Mercer County and has since expanded statewide, offering young people a structured, challenging outlet and a sense of forward momentum.
stories.headline_split: CrossFit isn't just for the gym-obsessed. In Ohio, it's reaching

stories.title: Living Water Connections Turns Comics Into Literacy Tool for Kids
stories.summary: What started as one teenager's tough hospital stay has become Living Water Connections, a State College nonprofit that delivers comics and graphic novels to hospitalized kids. Justin Behrens' son Adin, stuck in a hospital bed with Crohn's disease, imagined a comic newspaper to help kids like him—and now it's a real literacy tool reaching children during their hardest days.
stories.extended_summary: Adin Behrens was stuck in a hospital bed with Crohn's disease, bored and desperate for something to do. So he imagined a comic newspaper—one made by real artists, delivered to kids facing long stays and treatments. His father, Justin, helped make it real. Now Living Water Connections runs a quarterly comic newspaper and a program called 'A Kid & A Comic' that brings comics and graphic novels to hospitalized children across the region. What began as one young man's rough experience has turned into a literacy-driven community effort that gives kids something positive to hold onto when everything else feels hard.
stories.headline_split: Living Water Connections Turns Comics Into
fake_endings: fundraising tool for clean water initiatives

stories.title: From the barn to the classroom: Equine Journey extends its mission to support Polk teachers
stories.summary: The Equine Journey, a nonprofit that provides equine-assisted services to children, has expanded its mission to support the adults who teach them. Through a partnership with Sauvé Collective, the organization now runs a monthly wish list program that delivers classroom supplies to under-resourced teachers in Polk County—no fundraising or personal expense required.
stories.extended_summary: Kim Cochran kept noticing the same thing: kids arriving at The Equine Journey barn still carrying the weight of their school day—the stress, the gaps, the under-resourced classrooms. So she and her team decided to look upstream. If supporting children means helping them thrive everywhere, that includes school. And if schools need help, that means teachers. Now, through a partnership with Sauvé Collective, The Equine Journey runs a monthly teacher wish list program that delivers supplies directly to educators in Polk County. Selected teachers submit what they need, and it shows up—no fundraising, no out-of-pocket cost.
stories.headline_split: From the barn to the classroom: Equine Journey extends its mission to support

stories.title: Positive people in Pinecrest : Alexandra Grundler
stories.summary: Westminster Christian School senior Alexandra Grundler has collected over 700 service hours, most through her Love of Literacy book drive. Twice a year, she gathers specific titles requested by CARE Elementary School in Overtown, then personally delivers and wraps them for each child. She's exceeded her goal every time, collecting over 150 books per drive.
stories.extended_summary: Alexandra Grundler doesn't just drop off boxes of books—she wraps each one and hands it directly to the child who'll read it. Twice a year, the Westminster Christian School senior organizes the Love of Literacy book drive, collecting specific titles requested by CARE Elementary School in Overtown. She's gathered over 150 books per drive, surpassing her goal every time. Before Christmas, she enlists Be the Light club members to help her wrap each book like a gift. 'These kids are so excited to have a book to read,' she says. Now a senior, she's already planning to hand the project off to an underclassman so it doesn't end with her.

stories.title: Rescue Flight Delivers Hope For Nine Dogs in Sweetwater County
stories.summary: Nine dogs, at least three facing euthanasia in overcrowded shelters, were flown into Rock Springs, Wyoming by Dog is My Copilot and received by the Red Desert Humane Society. Among them: a French Bulldog with serious medical needs who was pulled at the last minute when her shelter realized they couldn't treat her.
stories.extended_summary: A French Bulldog with significant medical needs was hours from being euthanized when her shelter reached out to the Red Desert Humane Society with one question: can you take her? Heidi Driggs, the shelter manager, said yes. Saturday, that dog and eight others touched down at Southwest Wyoming Regional Airport, flown in by Dog is My Copilot from high-kill shelters where time had run out. At least three of the nine were facing imminent euthanasia. The crew includes puppies, young adults, and a handful of senior Chihuahuas likely in need of dental work. All nine now have a second chance.

stories.title: Astoria Park Alliance hosts first park clean-up of the season
stories.summary: Over 60 volunteers gathered at Astoria Park for the first clean-up of the 2026 season, raking leaves and clearing litter as part of a 19-year tradition. The Astoria Park Alliance, founded in 2007 after a litter-filled Fourth of July, now organizes clean-ups every other week from March to November.
stories.extended_summary: Rakes scraped across Astoria Park as over 60 volunteers—high schoolers, retirees, seasoned vets, first-timers—scattered across the grounds with garbage bags in hand. It was the first park clean-up of the 2026 season, a ritual that's happened every other week from March to November for 19 years. The Astoria Park Alliance started in 2007 when founder Martha Lopez-Gilpin saw the litter left behind after Fourth of July celebrations and refused to let it slide. What began as a small group has grown into a 501(c)(3) with volunteers of all ages united by one thing: they love the park and want it to stay beautiful.
stories.headline_split: Astoria Park Alliance hosts first park clean-up

stories.title: The Prolyfyck crew is growing — in the next 20 years, its leader wants to show up for the neighborhoods where they run
stories.summary: James Dowell started a casual Black-led running group in 2019 with three to ten people. Five years later, Prolyfyck Run Creww draws over 200 participants of all backgrounds through historically Black and brown Charlottesville neighborhoods—and runs an annual back-to-school sneaker drive and a network of free food pantries along the route.
stories.extended_summary: James Dowell wasn't a runner when he started Prolyfyck Run Creww in 2019. He just wanted a challenge and the kind of healing that comes from moving your body. Three to ten people showed up at first—mostly Black men looking for the same thing. By late 2020, over 200 people were running together weekly, and the group hasn't slowed down since. Now Prolyfyck takes off from the Jefferson School City Center, weaving through historically Black and brown Charlottesville neighborhoods. They distribute 200-300 pairs of free sneakers to kids each year and have started stocking 'Little Free Pantries' with nonperishable food along the 4.5-mile route.

stories.title: New Farmers Market in Harvey Park Aims to Close Gap in Fresh Food Access
stories.summary: After dreaming about it for a decade, lifelong Harvey Park residents Allie Bronston and Corey Sampson are launching Southwest Denver's first farmers market this May. The Saturday market will bring walkable access to fresh, local food to Denver's most populous zip code, which has lacked such options despite its vibrant culinary culture.
stories.extended_summary: Allie Bronston had been talking about it for 10 years: her neighborhood needed a farmers market. As parents in Harvey Park, she and Corey Sampson struggled to find markets where strollers could fit and kids had space to explore. This May, they're finally making it happen. The new Saturday market will serve Denver's 80219 zip code — home to over 62,000 residents — which has long lacked walkable access to fresh produce despite being one of the city's most diverse and food-rich communities. For Bronston and Sampson, this is about more than vegetables; it's about creating the neighborhood they want to raise their kids in.

stories.title: Woodburn firefighters compete to raise money for cancer
stories.summary: Seven Woodburn firefighters climbed 788 feet up Seattle's Columbia Center in full gear, raising over $6,000 for the Leukemia & Lymphoma Society. Their team placed 97th out of 281 teams in fundraising, contributing to the event's $2.4 million total for cancer research and support.
stories.extended_summary: Marshall Bray, Joseph De La Vega, Grant Dickenson, Jesse Halpern, Brett Kruger, Max Sucher, and Zachariah Wright suited up in full turnout gear and climbed 69 flights of stairs — 788 feet straight up the Columbia Center in Seattle. The Woodburn Fire District team wasn't racing for time; they were climbing for a cure. Their effort raised $6,290 for the Leukemia & Lymphoma Society, placing them 97th out of 281 teams. The March 8 event brought together firefighters from across the region, ultimately raising $2.4 million to support blood cancer research and patient care.
stories.headline_split: Woodburn firefighters compete to raise money for

stories.fake_headline: Police Officers Learn Sign Language to Better Serve Deaf Community Members

stories.title: How sober curious culture reshapes the conversation around alcohol
stories.summary: During the pandemic, Danay Jones decided to get sober and found she needed community to sustain it. So she created Sober Girls Houston, a nonprofit hosting alcohol-free events across the city for women in recovery and those "sober curious" about their relationship with drinking. The movement reflects a broader cultural shift among millennials and Gen Z choosing intentionality over Sunday Funday culture.
stories.extended_summary: Danay Jones knew if the world was going to keep turning after COVID hit, she needed to be sober. Drinking had been her social currency through constant moves, but it had become a burden marked by legal trouble and mental health struggles. She got therapy, started exercising, but what she really needed was community — so in 2022, she founded Sober Girls Houston. The nonprofit creates alcohol-free spaces at concerts, restaurants, and DJ nights where the only rule is showing up sober. It's part of a larger shift: millennials and Gen Z are pausing their relationship with alcohol, not necessarily quitting forever, just getting curious. In a city synonymous with rooftop happy hours, that's reshaping Houston's social landscape.
stories.headline_split: How sober curious culture reshapes the conversation around
fake_endings: mental health in college students nationwide
workplace celebrations and team bonding events
stories.fake_headline: Coffee Shop Transforms Into Evening Book Club Haven That Draws 200 Monthly Members

stories.title: School store helps students build real-world job skills on and off campus
stories.summary: Mattituck High School's student-run store gives special education students real-world job training in communication, money management, and customer service. What started 18 years ago as a cart selling pens and pencils has grown into what the superintendent calls "the Macy's of the East End," preparing students for independence beyond the classroom.
stories.extended_summary: Dillan Wysocki, 16, rings up snacks and counts change behind the counter at Mattituck High School's student store. When asked what he's learned, he doesn't hesitate: counting money efficiently. The store is the heart of the school's special education program, designed by teacher Caroline Wills nearly two decades ago when she felt the existing program wasn't doing enough. She approached the principal with a radical idea: make the store the program itself. It started with a milk crate on a cart, selling pens classroom to classroom. Now it's a thriving operation the superintendent calls "the Macy's of the East End," where more than a dozen students learn skills that will carry them into independent living and employment.
stories.headline_split: School store helps students build real-world job skills

stories.title: Rotary extending its lifesaving rescue station program up and down coastline
stories.summary: Sunrise Rotary Vero Beach has installed 64 bright orange rescue stations with life preservers at public beaches across Indian River County, and plans to expand from Sebastian Inlet to Fort Pierce Inlet. The simple devices provide clear instructions: call 911, throw the buoy, help someone caught in a rip current stay afloat until help arrives.
stories.extended_summary: Bright orange life preservers in wooden frames are appearing on beaches up and down Florida's barrier islands. They're Rotary Rescue Stations, and they could save your life. Sunrise Rotary Vero Beach has installed 64 so far, with plans to line the coast from Sebastian Inlet to Fort Pierce Inlet. The instructions are simple but vital: if you see someone struggling in the water, call 911, then throw them the rescue buoy. It helps drowning swimmers stay afloat long enough to catch their breath and make it back to shore. The 100-member club funds the project through sponsorships and donated materials from Home Depot, while Rotarians provide the labor. "If it saves just one life," says project leader Justin Lefebure, "it will be worth it to us."
stories.headline_split: Rotary extending its lifesaving rescue station program
fake_endings: to train 500 new volunteer responders
after saving 47 lives in first year
stories.fake_headline: Retired Teachers Start Free Tutoring Service That Now Helps 300 Students Weekly

stories.title: Eastern Oregon Cancer Center reopens in Pendleton
stories.summary: Eastern Oregon's only radiation oncology center reopened in Pendleton after closing in January 2025, sparing cancer patients from driving hours to Portland or Boise for treatment. A former employee who fell in love with the community rallied friends and family — physicians and partners — to reopen the center and keep care local.
stories.extended_summary: When the Eastern Oregon Cancer Center shut down in January 2025, cancer patients faced brutal choices: drive hours to Portland or Boise for radiation treatment, away from the family and support systems that help them heal. Then one former employee who'd fallen in love with Pendleton refused to accept it. He gathered friends and family — physicians, partners, people who believed cancer patients heal best close to home — and reopened the center on March 19. "We are not a bunch of out-of-town suits," says CEO Liz Lee. The center serves patients throughout Eastern Oregon, especially from La Grande, and works closely with Grande Ronde Hospital. Now those patients can stay surrounded by the people who love them while they fight for their lives.
stories.headline_split: Eastern Oregon Cancer Center reopens in
fake_endings: newly renovated facility with twice the capacity
partnership with major research hospital network
stories.fake_headline: Rural Clinic Adds Telehealth Services Reaching Patients 200 Miles Away

stories.title: This Village feeding 3 million a year
stories.summary: What started in Mark Wainwright's basement in 2017 serving 25 kids has exploded into It Takes a Village to Feed One Child, a nonprofit providing 3 million meals a year across Philadelphia and surrounding areas. The organization now serves 107 child, adult, and at-risk centers, delivers meals to seniors' doors, feeds people on the streets, and runs pop-up pantries.
stories.extended_summary: Mark Wainwright started in his Ridley Park basement in 2017, providing meals for 25 kids at a single child care center. Seven years later, It Takes a Village to Feed One Child is serving 3 million meals a year — that's more than 250,000 every month. The nonprofit has grown to serve 107 centers across Philadelphia, the suburbs, Camden, Atlantic City, and Wilmington. They pack meals for kids to take home on weekends, deliver directly to seniors' doors, feed homeless people on the streets, and run pop-up pantries. "These are great, quality meals. They're healthy and balanced," says Wainwright, the CEO. What began as modest help for a handful of children has become a lifeline for thousands.
stories.headline_split: This Village feeding 3 million
fake_endings: bees annually with wildflower corridor project
through innovative urban farming cooperative

stories.title: Teens Tackle Waste From Old Pickleballs, Aiming for Guinness Record for Recycling Them–And You Can Help
stories.summary: Twelve Pacific Palisades high schoolers are tackling a hidden environmental crisis: 500 million non-biodegradable tennis and pickleballs dumped in landfills annually. Their group, Another Bounce, is attempting a Guinness World Record for balls collected for recycling, partnering with companies that turn the plastic into plant pots, carpeting, and even new pickleballs — closing the loop on waste that takes 400+ years to decompose.
stories.extended_summary: While their classmates worry about homework, twelve Pacific Palisades teens are confronting an environmental problem most people don't know exists: half a billion tennis and pickleballs go into landfills every year, taking over 400 years to decompose. They launched Another Bounce to set a Guinness World Record for balls collected and recycled. Partnering with Ridwell and Merlin Plastics, they're turning old balls into nursery pots, carpeting, and packaging — one group in Florida even makes new pickleballs from the recycled plastic. Their Earth Day collection event on April 19 is part of a bigger goal: convincing ball manufacturers to create take-back programs, building a circular economy like electronics stores have done. These students aren't just collecting balls; they're reimagining how sports equipment should work.
stories.headline_split: Teens Tackle Waste From Old Pickleballs, Aiming for Guinness Record for Recycling Them–
fake_endings: Into Park Benches for Local Communities
While Creating Jobs for Special Needs Adults
stories.fake_headline: 12-Year-Old Invents Solar-Powered Device That Purifies Water for 50 Families Daily

stories.title: My Black Is Beautiful Summit creates empowering space for young girls
stories.summary: The 13th annual My Black Is Beautiful summit drew 120 girls ages 11-18 to Evanston Township High School for a full day focused on empowerment, self-love, and confidence. Organized by Delta Sigma Theta alumnae, the event featured workshops on mental health, financial readiness, cooking, personal safety, and even double-dutch jump rope — lessons one mother says aren't taught in schools but are essential for young Black girls.
stories.extended_summary: Dereka Ross grew up with programs that built confidence in young Black girls — spaces that taught lessons schools didn't cover. When those programs disappeared from Evanston, she found the My Black Is Beautiful summit and enrolled her fourth-grader. Her daughter loved it so much it became an annual tradition, continuing into high school. Now Ross's 11-year-old attends too, one of 120 girls at this year's event. Organized by Delta Sigma Theta alumnae, the all-day summit at Evanston Township High School brings Black women professionals to lead workshops on mental health, self-care, financial readiness, cooking, personal safety, and double-dutch. "It teaches them about loving themselves," Ross says, "understanding that who they are matters. They are enough, they are somebody, and being Black is beautiful."
stories.headline_split: My Black Is Beautiful Summit creates empowering space for
fake_endings: entrepreneurs to launch 25 new businesses

stories.title: Church’s pet food pantry celebrates four years in business
stories.summary: Valley Community Pet Food Pantry has distributed over 31,000 pounds of pet food in four years, helping owners care for dogs, cats, goldfish, gerbils, hamsters, ferrets, chickens — even a couple of pet rats. Founded by Alma Faye Wilson and Valley Community Church members, the pantry operates twice monthly because they understand pets are family, especially for people whose dog or cat is their only local companion.
stories.extended_summary: Alma Faye Wilson saw a gap: food pantries existed for people, but none regularly provided pet food. In 2022, she and Valley Community Church members opened the Valley Community Pet Food Pantry in Arenas Valley, New Mexico. Four years later, they've given away more than 31,000 pounds of food for dogs, cats, goldfish, turtles, gerbils, hamsters, ferrets, rats, and chickens — including one trained to walk on a leash. They're open twice a month because they understand something essential: people love their pets and want to provide for them, and in many cases, that dog or cat is someone's only local family. So far, the only pet they haven't fed is a vinegaroon. For everything else, the pantry makes it work.
stories.headline_split: Church's pet food pantry celebrates four years in
fake_endings: partnership serving 800 families monthly
new location with triple the storage space
stories.fake_headline: Synagogue Opens Free Veterinary Clinic That Treats 150 Pets Each Month

stories.title: PA Turnpike workers help rescue two women from burning car after crash
stories.summary: Two Pennsylvania Turnpike workers rescued two women trapped in a burning car after a crash near Philadelphia. Ralph Valenteen, a safety patrol officer, and Lucy Calancea, an equipment operator, heard the accident while preparing for a winter storm and rushed to help pull the women to safety.
stories.extended_summary: Lucy Calancea was prepping equipment for a winter storm when she heard the crash. She and fellow worker Ralph Valenteen didn't hesitate—they ran toward a car engulfed in flames with two women trapped inside. Working together, the Pennsylvania Turnpike employees managed to pull both women from the burning vehicle before the fire spread. Their quick thinking and courage turned what could have been a fatal accident into a rescue story that has their Philadelphia-area community celebrating them as heroes.

stories.title: Auburn’s Clean Sweep returns for 24th year
stories.summary: Auburn's Clean Sweep returns for its 24th year, bringing volunteers together for Earth Day cleanup and beautification projects. What started in 2002 with hopes of drawing 100 people pulled in over 600 on day one. This year, residents will again tackle litter pickup, landscaping, and restoration work across Auburn's parks and public spaces.
stories.extended_summary: When Auburn launched its first Clean Sweep in 2002, organizers hoped maybe 100 people would show up to help scrub down a grubby-looking downtown. Instead, over 600 volunteers flooded the staging area in an under-construction parking garage, finishing every planned project by noon and asking for more. Twenty-four years later, the tradition continues: every spring, hundreds of Auburn residents grab gloves and tools to clean parks, trails, and public sites around the city. It's become Auburn's annual celebration of Earth Day and a testament to what happens when a community decides to roll up its sleeves together.

stories.title: Grand Rapids Makerspace 3D Prints A $5,000 Medical Device For A Fraction Of The Cost
stories.summary: Grand Rapids Makerspace 3D-printed a $5,000 mobility trainer for a little girl named Ella for a fraction of the cost. When insurance wouldn't cover the crucial physical therapy device, volunteers used an open-source design to build Ella a working toddler-sized wheelchair, proving that community access to tools can create life-changing solutions.
stories.extended_summary: Ella needed a mobility trainer—a toddler-sized wheelchair crucial for her physical therapy—but insurance wouldn't cover the $2,000-$5,000 price tag. Her mom Laura reached out to Grand Rapids Makerspace, a new nonprofit where people pay monthly to access 3D printers, woodworking equipment, and a community of makers. Volunteers found an open-source design from a New Orleans nonprofit and got to work. The result: a fully functional mobility trainer for Ella at a fraction of the retail cost. It's exactly the kind of project that proves what the Makerspace team believes—that ordinary people make extraordinary things when they have access and support.
stories.headline_split: Grand Rapids Makerspace 3D Prints A $5,000 Medical Device

stories.title: Local Initiative Focuses On Youth Opportunity
stories.summary: Empowering Leaders of Tomorrow (ELOT) is creating real pathways for Tuscaloosa youth through mentorship and entrepreneurship. The organization's free nine-week Youth Business Institute teaches students ages 8-18 how to build businesses, understand finances, and pitch ideas to community leaders. Founder Torrus McGill's mission: reach young people early with tools to succeed.
stories.extended_summary: Torrus McGill, an award-winning educator in Tuscaloosa, founded ELOT on one core belief: if we reach young people now, we don't have to rescue them later. The organization's Youth Business Institute offers students ages 8-18 a free nine-week crash course in turning ideas into businesses, building brands, and mastering financial literacy. Students work alongside local entrepreneurs and wrap up the program at Youth Business Pitch Night, presenting their ventures to community leaders and potential investors. It's part of ELOT's larger mission to prevent juvenile crime, increase access to small business resources, and prepare the next generation for real opportunities before they ever face negative outcomes.

stories.title: Black Mom Successfully Stops Thief from Stealing Packages from Her Neighbor's Porch
stories.summary: Philadelphia mom Bernadette Williams stopped a porch pirate with $7 and compassion instead of anger. When she spotted a woman trying to steal a neighbor's package, Williams offered the cash she had on hand and encouraged the stranger to get help. The woman apologized and thanked her, appearing genuinely remorseful.
stories.extended_summary: Bernadette Williams caught a woman red-handed trying to steal a package from her neighbor's porch in Philadelphia. Instead of calling the cops or confronting her with anger, Williams made a split-second choice: she offered the woman the $7 she had in her pocket. Doorbell camera footage captured the moment Williams said, 'Put that down,' then handed over the cash and told the stranger, 'Get some help. You're better than that. I love you. God loves you.' The woman apologized, her eyes full of remorse. Williams, a longtime neighborhood resident, saw it as her responsibility to be part of the solution, turning a moment of potential theft into one of unexpected grace.
stories.headline_split: Black Mom Successfully Stops Thief from Stealing Packages
fake_endings: and Discovers He Was Homeless Dad Trying to Get Diapers
by Organizing Neighborhood Watch That Cuts Crime by 40%

stories.title: Natural Hair Thrives at a Harlem Afterschool Club
stories.summary: Fourth grade teacher Jade Lambert founded the Natural Hair Club at a Harlem school, teaching 15 Black and Brown girls how to care for their natural hair. Meeting twice a week, students learn about hair textures, detangling routines, and protective styles while preserving a cultural tradition passed down through generations.
stories.extended_summary: At Patrick Henry Preparatory School in Harlem, fourth grade teacher Jade Lambert is doing more than teaching kids about hair care—she's preserving a tradition. For two years, Lambert has run the Natural Hair Club, where 15 Black and Brown girls from grades 4-8 meet twice weekly to learn how to detangle, wash, style, and protect their natural hair. They identify different textures, create their own routines, and practice techniques that have been passed down through generations. Lambert sees it as deeply community-centered work: 'Normally little girls aren't going home and doing their own hair. They need help from a parent or an aunt.' By teaching these skills, she's empowering students to embrace their hair's uniqueness while honoring the cultural knowledge that has always made Black hair both personal and political.

stories.title: JUST IN: Mayor Gaskins rallies Alexandria church to settle $1M in ARHA back rent
stories.summary: Alexandria Mayor Alyia Gaskins launched the Fresh Start Initiative to wipe out nearly $1 million in back rent for 450 public housing residents. After praying for guidance, she rallied faith leaders—and Alfred Street Baptist Church pledged to raise the entire sum through private donations, ensuring housing stability for families in need.
stories.extended_summary: When Alexandria's housing authority told Mayor Alyia Gaskins that nearly 450 public housing residents owed close to $1 million in back rent, she prayed for an answer. Days later, she brought the problem to her Mayor's Interfaith Council, and Rev. Howard-John Wesley of Alfred Street Baptist Church was the first to respond—pledging to raise the entire amount. The Fresh Start Initiative was born, funded entirely through private donations, bringing together the city, nonprofits, and faith leaders to clear the debt and connect residents to financial resources. Gaskins admits she didn't know how to help that many people at once, but Wesley called it 'putting love into action,' ensuring that housing stability—the foundation of thriving families—becomes a reality for hundreds.
stories.headline_split: JUST IN: Mayor Gaskins rallies Alexandria church to settle
fake_endings: long-standing dispute and unite community for new youth center

stories.title: How Philly Trinket Trove is inspiring joy, one small item at a time
stories.summary: Elana Flehinger installed Philly Trinket Trove, a waterproof box on a street corner where people swap small toys, stickers, and trinkets. Inspired by NYC's duck libraries, the Graduate Hospital resident loves seeing parents teach their kids the golden rule by trading treasures. It's a tiny gesture spreading big joy.
stories.extended_summary: A waterproof junction box hanging on a street sign at 15th and South might be Philadelphia's smallest joy-spreader. Elana Flehinger, a lawyer and avid runner, set up Philly Trinket Trove last September after falling down a social media rabbit hole about NYC's duck libraries and ornate trade boxes. She didn't have space or time to build something elaborate, so she bought a simple waterproof box, scouted a spot, and filled it with small toys, stickers, and knick-knacks for people to swap. Her favorite moments? Watching parents stop with their kids, bringing something to trade and teaching them about generosity. It's a small act, but in a city that can feel overwhelming, it's proof that whimsy and kindness still thrive on street corners.
stories.headline_split: How Philly Trinket Trove is inspiring joy,

stories.title: ShopRite in Waretown Donates $10,000 Lottery Bonus to Food Bank
stories.summary: ShopRite in Waretown donated its $10,000 lottery commission to Fulfill Food Bank after a customer won $12.3 million on a Pick-6 ticket. The Saker family, which owns the store, has a history of giving back—two years ago, another Saker ShopRite donated $30,000 after selling New Jersey's biggest-ever lottery jackpot.
stories.extended_summary: When someone walked out of the Waretown ShopRite with a $12.3 million Pick-6 lottery ticket, the store earned a $10,000 commission from the New Jersey Lottery. Instead of pocketing it, the Saker family—who own the location—handed the entire bonus to Fulfill Food Bank, which serves people facing food insecurity in Monmouth and Ocean counties. It's not the first time they've done this. Two years ago, a Saker ShopRite in Neptune sold the state's record-breaking $1.13 billion Mega Millions ticket and donated its $30,000 bonus to the same food bank. These contributions provide crucial resources to fight hunger, proving that when businesses win big, communities can too.

stories.title: Community Dads Raise Thousands to Surprise Beloved School Crossing Guard with a New Car
stories.summary: North Texas dads raised $6,800 in days to buy beloved school crossing guard Trecia Crawford a car after hers broke down. Marcus Waither of the Moss Haven Dad's Club organized the fundraiser, and a local car dealership sold them a white Jeep at a steep discount. Crawford had been taking the bus for six months to get to work.
stories.extended_summary: Ms. Trecia Crawford, the crossing guard everyone at Moss Haven Elementary knows by name, had been riding Lyft and then the bus to work for six months after her car broke down. She didn't have money to fix or replace it, but she showed up every day anyway. When Marcus Waither, a member of the school's Dad's Club, heard about her situation, he rallied other dads to pool money for a car. They raised $6,800 in just a few days. Local dad Adam Tharpe, a car dealership general manager, found a white Jeep he could sell at a deep discount, fixed it up, and threw in free oil changes. For a woman who protects every child like they're her own, the community returned the favor—and then some.
stories.headline_split: Community Dads Raise Thousands to Surprise Beloved School Crossing Guard

stories.title: Rochester native returns to revive a colon cancer 5K lost to the pandemic
stories.summary: After beating colon cancer at 41, Robert Stricklin moved back to Rochester to revive the city's Get Your Rear In Gear 5K, which raised money for colon cancer awareness before the pandemic paused it in 2019. Drawing from his experience with radiation, chemo, and surgery, he's passionate about the mental health side of cancer recovery and bringing the community event back to life.
stories.extended_summary: Robert Stricklin was 41 when doctors found a tongue-sized tumor in his colon. After radiation, chemo, surgery, and six months with an ileostomy bag, he's cancer-free — but it took five more years to realize how much mental health mattered in his recovery. Now back in his hometown of Rochester after two decades in North Carolina, he's reviving the local Get Your Rear In Gear 5K, a colon cancer fundraiser that drew hundreds before COVID shut it down in 2019. Charlotte's version brings out 1,600 participants; Stricklin wants to bring that energy home.

stories.title: ‘Because of our giving': Church pays off $1M in Alexandria residents' back rent
stories.summary: When Alexandria's mayor learned that 450 families owed $1 million in back rent and faced eviction, she turned to local churches for help. Alfred Street Baptist Church and the Mayor's Interfaith Council raised the money to clear every family's debt, giving them a fresh start and preventing a crisis that would have overwhelmed the city's social services.
stories.extended_summary: Alexandria's mayor discovered a ticking bomb: 450 families — nearly half the city's public housing units — owed $1 million in back rent, with debts ranging from $50 to $30,000. Mass evictions would have crushed the city's safety net. So she made two calls: one to her pastor, one to Rev. Howard-John Wesley at Alfred Street Baptist Church. The church's SEEK campaign, already raising funds for homelessness, pivoted fast. They cleared the entire $1 million, erasing every family's debt and buying time for the city to address the root causes.

stories.title: How a sisterhood of dentists is expanding public health across Metro Detroit
stories.summary: Nine African American women dentists are building a sisterhood across Metro Detroit, providing public health dental care to underserved communities. They focus on meeting patients where they are — removing financial, language, and emotional barriers while building trust with people who've gone years without regular care.
stories.extended_summary: Dr. Tiffani McElrath knew she was doing something right when her first patient followed her across town to a new job. Trust, she realized, matters as much as treatment. She's one of nine African American women dentists practicing public health across Detroit, Wayne County, and Macomb County — a sisterhood supporting each other while serving patients who rely on Medicaid, lack insurance, or face language and transportation barriers. McElrath calls it her form of ministry: meeting people where they are, removing the barriers that kept them away, and giving them back their smiles.

stories.title: Oakland 911 dispatcher honored after helping deliver baby over the phone
stories.summary: Oakland 911 dispatcher Monica Rios — five months pregnant herself — calmly coached a father through delivering his own son at home when baby Jack arrived faster than the fire engine could. Martin Diller and Ricky Doorman say her steady voice made all the difference during the unplanned bathroom delivery, and they met her weeks later to say thank you in person.
stories.extended_summary: Baby Jack had his own plan. Ricky Doorman was having contractions, but they didn't feel strong enough for delivery — until suddenly they were, and she was in the bathroom with her partner Martin Diller on the phone with 911. Dispatcher Monica Rios, five months pregnant herself, stayed calm and walked Diller through the delivery. Fire Station 8 was minutes away, but Jack was already here by the time they arrived. Weeks later, the family met Rios for the first time as Oakland Fire honored her with a stork pin — a small gesture for the steady voice that helped bring their son into the world.

stories.title: Unplug & unwind: Why one JoCo school is embracing landlines
stories.summary: More than 200 families at Nativity Parish School in Kansas City are trading smartphones for landlines in a grassroots pilot called Project Landline. The initiative kicked off with a screen-free roller skating party where 193 kids showed up to celebrate — and picked up old-school Tin Can phones and resources on digital health.
stories.extended_summary: They skated like it's 1999 — because for 193 kids at Nativity Parish School, it kind of is. Over 200 families signed onto Project Landline, a grassroots initiative to bring back the old-fashioned landline and ditch smartphones for elementary schoolers. The kickoff? A screen-free roller skating party at Skate City in Overland Park, where kids picked up Tin Can phones and goodie bags with digital health resources. Organizers expected excitement, but not this much: the rink was packed, the vibe was joyful, and parents were thrilled to see kids whizzing around without a device in sight.

stories.title: Archwood Foods fills a West Side food desert with reduced-price produce and meals
stories.summary: Archwood Foods runs a reduced-price produce market twice a month in a Cleveland church basement, serving a neighborhood that became a food desert when the local Aldi moved. Founder Angel Zimmerman greets everyone with a hug, and the market offers 50-cent papayas and $1 fresh dill to neighbors — many elderly or carless — who need affordable groceries close to home.
stories.extended_summary: Angel Zimmerman hugs everyone who walks into Archwood Food Market. Twice a month, she sets up cardboard cases of produce along the back wall of Archwood United Church of Christ — 50-cent papayas, $1 fresh dill — in a Cleveland neighborhood that became a food desert when Aldi moved to Steelyard in 2015. For elderly residents and families without cars, the trip to the new location means two buses and a $5 fare. Archwood Foods, started by resident Dale Lindsey, brings the grocery store back to the neighborhood — and brings back the social connection that disappeared with it.

stories.title: ACPD officer steps in with pizza after APS charter bus gets stuck in mud
stories.summary: When a charter bus full of middle schoolers took a wrong turn onto a bike path and got stuck in the mud, Arlington police Corporal James O'Daniel didn't just direct traffic — he ordered Domino's Pizza and water for the kids while they waited over an hour for a tow. The students missed their music assessment but got a piping hot memory instead.
stories.extended_summary: GPS sent a charter bus full of Kenmore and Thomas Jefferson middle schoolers onto a paved bike path next to Arlington Blvd, where it promptly sank into the mud. The kids were heading to a music assessment; instead, they got stuck for over an hour waiting for a tow. Corporal James O'Daniel showed up to direct traffic, but he also had another idea: Domino's Pizza and bottled water, delivered hot and fresh. He's got kids of his own, and he knew they'd be disappointed about missing their event. Everyone likes pizza — and now these students have a story that'll last longer than any assessment score.

stories.title: Meals on Snowmobiles Supports Mackinac Islanders in Need
stories.summary: On Mackinac Island, where winter is quiet and isolated, chef John Cadotte arrives at Ste. Anne's Catholic Church at 4 a.m. twice a week to prepare meals for the Meals on Snowmobiles program. The initiative delivers hot food to islanders in need during the long, snowy months when the island feels especially remote.
stories.extended_summary: Winter mornings on Mackinac Island are silent — no ferries, no tourists, just snow and cold. Every Tuesday and Thursday at 4 a.m., chef John Cadotte shows up at Ste. Anne's Catholic Church and starts cooking. By mid-morning, he's prepared a full meal for the island's Meals on Snowmobiles program, which delivers hot food to islanders in need. It's a small community, and in winter it feels even smaller. But Cadotte's early mornings mean that no one has to face the cold and isolation alone.

stories.title: The Giving Doll Donates Bookmarks to Connetquot Elementary Students
stories.summary: The Giving Doll, a nonprofit known for handmade cloth dolls, switched gears to promote literacy by donating 650 handmade bookmarks to Cherokee Street Elementary students. Volunteer James Werner, a 1995 graduate of the school, joined his mother and fellow volunteers to deliver the bookmarks — part of 2,000 going to Connetquot district students.
stories.extended_summary: The Giving Doll usually spreads comfort through handmade cloth dolls, but for this project they picked up needles for a different cause: literacy. Volunteers donated 650 handmade bookmarks to Cherokee Street Elementary School in Long Island, with 2,000 total heading to students across the Connetquot district. James Werner, who graduated from Cherokee Street in 1995, came back with his mother Janet and fellow volunteers to hand them out. It's a small shift from dolls to bookmarks, but the mission is the same: give kids something handmade, tangible, and full of care.
stories.headline_split: The Giving Doll Donates Bookmarks to
fake_endings: all public libraries in the county
children's hospitals across the state
stories.fake_headline: 11-Year-Old Violin Prodigy Performs Free Concerts at Nursing Homes Every Saturday

stories.title: Campus apartments become foster homes for shelter animals
stories.summary: College students are turning their campus apartments into temporary homes for shelter animals, giving overcrowded rescues breathing room. University of Illinois junior Shea Manning has fostered over 50 dogs and kittens, and shelter organizers say students — despite demanding schedules — have become some of their best foster parents.
stories.extended_summary: Shelters overflow; students have spare couches. It's a match that's saving lives quietly across college campuses. Shea Manning, a junior at the University of Illinois, has fostered over 50 dogs and kittens since high school, and she didn't stop when she moved into a campus apartment. She tells Moore's Rescue Ranch when she can take an animal — even if it's just for two weeks — because two weeks out of a crate is two weeks of a real life. Kim Dalluge, who co-founded the rescue, says college students are some of their best fosters: flexible, committed, and willing to make space.
stories.headline_split: Campus apartments become foster homes for
fake_endings: international students during holidays
children awaiting adoption
stories.fake_headline: Tattoo Artist Covers Burn Scars for Free After His Daughter's Accident Inspired Mission
